text.title: Cross-Channel CPA Rollup Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn cross-channel attribution from a vendor-supplied fiction into a defensible internal rollup.

rule.statement: The rollup MUST use ONE documented attribution model — default: last non-direct click within a 30-day click window + a separate view-through column with a 1-day post-impression window. Each channel's self-reported conversion count is recorded for reference but does NOT enter the rollup CPA. The attribution policy is committed and dated.

rationale.source: Adopting one model is the only way to compare channels; cf. Forrester attribution research

rule.rationale: Vendor-reported CPA sums to > total conversions because every platform claims credit. A single internal model — even imperfect — produces a comparison that adds up.

rule.statement: Channel cost MUST be reported as gross spend: ad platform billing + agency/management fees + creative production amortization + any platform-specific premium. Net-of-rebate spend is reported as a separate column. CPA is computed with gross.
rule.rationale: Agency-fee-less CPA reports a number the business does not actually pay. Using gross makes the CPA reflect real economics; net is a vanity input for negotiation.

rule.statement: Conversions MUST be deduplicated by a unifying customer ID (email hash, user ID, anonymized identifier). A conversion counts once across all channels. If the user clicked Meta then Google then converted, the rollup attributes per model — not once per channel.

rationale.source: Identity-resolution practice in adtech (LiveRamp, server-side stitching)

rule.rationale: Without deduplication, the rollup over-counts conversions and under-estimates CPA. Identity stitching closes the loop.

rule.statement: Organic + referral + direct traffic MUST be reported on the rollup as separate rows with their own cost basis (typically zero or the cost of the SEO/content investment amortized). Paid CPA is reported against the incremental conversions over the organic baseline, not against gross conversions.

rationale.source: Incrementality testing research (Meta lift studies, geo-experiments)

rule.rationale: Paid channels frequently claim conversions that would have happened organically. Reporting the organic baseline forces honest incrementality conversations.

rule.statement: Beyond single-touch CPA, the rollup MUST include a 90-day cohort retention column per channel: of the customers acquired in week N, how many were still active at day 30/60/90. A channel with low CPA and low retention is over-credited; one with higher CPA and high retention may be under-credited.

rationale.source: LTV/CAC framework; cf. David Skok, SaaS metrics

rule.rationale: CPA in isolation rewards channels that source low-quality users. The cohort lens surfaces the difference and informs budget allocation correctly.

example.title: Rollup row using all rules
example.code: Week: 2026-W20 Channel: Meta paid Gross spend: $4,800 (incl. $600 agency fee) Attributed conversions (last non-direct, 30d): 32 Self-reported conversions: 51 (recorded, not used) CPA: $150 ($4,800 / 32) View-through count (1d): 18 (caveat: lift untested) Day-30 cohort retention: 71% (vs Google 83%, organic 84%)
example.note: Single model, gross spend, dedup, view-through separated, cohort lens included.

example.title: Per-channel claim aggregated
example.code: Meta reports: 51 conversions, $4.8k spend, CPA $94 Google reports: 42 conversions, $3.2k spend, CPA $76 Combined CPA: $86 (51+42=93 conversions, $8k spend)
example.note: Total conversions claimed (93) exceeds actual (likely 50-60 unique). CPA is fictional. Violates r1, r3.
